tool.name: create_vulnerability_report
tool.description:
Create a vulnerability report for a discovered security issue. One report per vulnerability. Deduplication is automatic — if rejected as duplicate, do not re-submit. IMPORTANT: if the same report fails validation 3+ times (missing params), stop retrying and try a different approach.
REQUIRED: title, description, impact, target, technical_analysis. OPTIONAL (auto-filled if omitted): poc_description (=description), poc_script_code (only required for LIKELY/VERIFIED), remediation_steps (= "See impact + technical analysis for remediation guidance."), cvss_breakdown (auto-calculated from severity if provided, otherwise estimated LOW). Optional: endpoint, method, parameter, cve, cwe, code_locations (MANDATORY for white-box: include fix_before/fix_after), severity (auto-calculates cvss_breakdown).
Report rules: Do NOT include local paths, internal tools, agents, sandboxes, models, system prompts, connection issues, or stack traces. Tone: formal, objective, third-person. Structure per finding: Overview, Severity/CVSS, Affected asset, Technical details, PoC (repro + code), Impact, Remediation. Numbered steps only in PoC and remediation.
Validation quick wins: - cwe must be exact ID format like "CWE-89" (NOT "89" or "CWE-89: SQL Injection"). - If you already validated exploitation but are short on time, set confidence=SUSPECTED and provide concrete technical_analysis (payload sent + observed response). - severity can replace cvss_breakdown for fast valid submissions (e.g., severity=HIGH).

parameter.name: title
parameter.description: Clear, specific title (e.g., "SQL Injection in Login Parameter"). But not too long. Don't mention CVE number in the title.

parameter.name: description
parameter.description: Comprehensive description of the vulnerability and how it was discovered

parameter.name: impact
parameter.description: Impact assessment: what attacker can do, business risk, data at risk

parameter.name: target
parameter.description: Affected target: URL, domain, or Git repository

parameter.name: technical_analysis
parameter.description: Technical explanation of the vulnerability mechanism and root cause

parameter.name: poc_description
parameter.description: Step-by-step instructions to reproduce the vulnerability. If omitted, the description field is used.

parameter.name: poc_script_code
parameter.description: Actual proof of concept code, exploit, payload, or script that demonstrates the vulnerability. Python code. Only REQUIRED for LIKELY or VERIFIED confidence. Can be omitted for SUSPECTED.

parameter.name: remediation_steps
parameter.description: Specific, actionable steps to fix the vulnerability. If omitted, a generic remediation message is used.

parameter.name: cvss_breakdown
parameter.description: CVSS 3.1 base score as nested XML. If omitted, auto-calculated from severity: CRITICAL=9.8, HIGH=8.9, MEDIUM=6.5, LOW=3.7, INFO=0.1. You can also provide plain vector like "AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:N".

parameter.name: severity
parameter.description: Severity tier: CRITICAL, HIGH, MEDIUM, LOW, INFO. If provided, auto-calculates cvss_breakdown and cvss_score. Overrides any provided cvss_breakdown.

parameter.name: endpoint
parameter.description: API endpoint(s) or URL path(s) (e.g., "/api/login") - for web vulnerabilities, or Git repository path(s) - for code vulnerabilities

parameter.name: method
parameter.description: HTTP method(s) (GET, POST, etc.) - for web vulnerabilities.

parameter.name: parameter
parameter.description: The specific HTTP parameter, header, or query string variable that carries the vulnerability (e.g., "q", "id", "username", "Authorization"). If the endpoint contains a query string (e.g., "/search?q=test"), extract the parameter name from it (e.g., "q"). If the vulnerable input is in a request body (e.g., POST JSON), include the field name (e.g., "username"). If there is no specific parameter (e.g., SSRF via URL field), omit this field.

parameter.name: cve
parameter.description: CVE identifier. ONLY the ID, e.g. "CVE-2024-1234" — do NOT include the name or description. You must be 100% certain of the exact CVE number. Do NOT guess, approximate, or hallucinate CVE IDs. If web_search is available, use it to verify the CVE exists and matches this vulnerability. If you cannot verify it, omit this field entirely.

parameter.name: cwe
parameter.description: CWE identifier. ONLY the ID (e.g., "CWE-89"). Do NOT include the name or parenthetical. Use web_search to verify. Prefer the most specific child CWE over broad parents (e.g., CWE-89 over CWE-74). Common: CWE-89 SQLi, CWE-79 XSS, CWE-918 SSRF, CWE-352 CSRF, CWE-78 OS Command Injection, CWE-94 Code Injection, CWE-287 Improper Auth, CWE-862/863 Authorization, CWE-601 Open Redirect, CWE-502 XXE, CWE-22 Path Traversal, CWE-798 Hard-coded Credentials, CWE-434 Unsafe Upload. Omit if uncertain.

parameter.name: code_locations
parameter.description: MANDATORY for white-box testing. Nested XML list of code locations. Each location has: file (path relative to repo root, no leading slash), start_line, end_line (1-based inclusive range), snippet (optional), label (optional), fix_before (verbatim vulnerable code), fix_after (corrected code). fix_before/fix_after are LITERAL line-range replacements — fix_before must match source exactly character-for-character. Do NOT guess line numbers. Multi-part fixes need separate location entries. Omit this field for black-box testing.

location.label: Unsanitized input used in SQL query (sink)
location.fix_after: const query = 'SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = $1'; const result = await db.query(query, [id]);

parameter.name: confidence
parameter.description:
Confidence tier for this finding. Must be one of: - SUSPECTED: Detection signal only, not independently verified (use this if unsure) - LIKELY: You personally tested with a crafted payload and observed a confirming response - VERIFIED: Reserved for automatic PoC replay — do NOT set this manually
If omitted, defaults to LIKELY. See CONFIDENCE TIERS in execution guidelines for rules.

returns.description: Response containing: - On success: success=true, message, report_id, severity, cvss_score - On duplicate detection: success=false, message (with duplicate info), duplicate_of (ID), duplicate_title, confidence (0-1), reason (why it's a duplicate)

function.parameter: Authentication Bypass via SQL Injection in Login Endpoint
Login accepts crafted SQL payload in the username field and issues an authenticated session.
Unauthenticated attacker can bypass authentication and access protected user data.
Sent POST /login with username payload "' OR 1=1 --" and arbitrary password. Response returned HTTP 200 with auth token and authenticated user context, confirming server-side SQL query manipulation.
Sensitive Information Exposure via Stack Trace Leak
Invalid endpoint requests disclose backend stack traces and internal file paths.
Leaked internals improve attacker recon and exploit targeting.
Request sent: GET /invalid. Observed response: HTTP 500 with backend stack-trace lines and internal file path disclosure. This confirms sensitive error disclosure in production responses.
Classic SQL injection via the username parameter on the login endpoint.
The username value is interpolated directly into a SQL query without parameterization.
1. POST /login with username: admin' OR '1'='1' -- 2. Observe successful authentication without valid credentials.
import requests r = requests.post("https://example.com/login", json={"username": "admin' OR '1'='1' --", "password": "x"}) print(r.status_code, r.text[:200])
Use parameterized queries / prepared statements. Never interpolate user input into SQL strings.
